matches.match_rationale: The core pain is a multi-step workflow: receive partial payment, match against outstanding instalments, update family account state, recalculate downstream balances, flag exceptions. This is exactly the branching, multi-system orchestration pattern task automation agents handle.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An automation agent ingests payment receipts, applies configurable allocation rules (oldest-first, pro-rata, manual override), updates the ledger state, and queues exception cases for human review.

matches.match_rationale: A key pain mechanism is the drift between accounting records and actual receipts. Anomaly detection can flag accounts where the expected payment pattern diverges significantly from recorded state.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A model trained on historical payment sequences identifies accounts where the gap between expected and recorded balance exceeds a threshold, generating a prioritized exceptions list.

matches.match_rationale: When a family requests a schedule change due to hardship, staff must manually draft updated payment agreements and family-facing notifications. LLM text generation can produce these automatically.
matches.opportunity_sketch: After a schedule change is approved and recalculated, the system generates a personalized family notification summarising the new plan, outstanding balance, and next due dates.

matches.match_rationale: Instalment tracking data lives in spreadsheets, fragmented mobile apps, and manual receipts with inconsistent schemas. AI-assisted ETL can normalize these into a unified ledger schema.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A transformation layer ingests per-campus payment exports in varying formats, maps them to a canonical schema, and loads them into a central reconciliation database.
